news_communities.title: Road rage fight caught on camera in Lynn
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain and Matt Fortin
news_communities.summary:
A man was hospitalized after a road rage incident Friday morning in Lowell, Massachusetts.

The incident escalated into an assault at a car wash on Route 1A. It’s being investigated by Massachusetts State Police, who responded shortly before 9:30 a.m..

Video from the scene showed traffic at a standstill as several people got out of their cars and started fighting in the roadway.

Two men were seen throwing punches while others tried to break it up.

A third man can be seen knocking someone to the ground.

“People have an ego,” said Jay Lopez, a customer at the car wash. “When somebody doesn’t back down, they have like, ‘Oh, I’m not going back down either.’ And then they just have to go back and forth until somebody goes to sleep.”

He feared it could have gone worse.

“It could have been a gun,” he said. “All you’ve got to do is mind your business. Drink water, mind your business.”

State police said the man who was knocked down suffered a laceration. He was taken to an area hospital.

No arrests have been made.

The case remains under investigation.

news_communities.title: 3 charged with human trafficking at Salem spas
news_communities.summary:
Police in Salem, Massachusetts, said three people are facing charges after a monthslong human trafficking investigation into several businesses.

Authorities said they investigated suspected trafficking and prostitution-related activities at businesses advertising “bodywork,” “reflexology” and “spa” services over the last three months.

Detectives executed search warrants Friday at businesses including New Custom Body Work and Red Rose Reflexology, both on Boston Street, police said Wednesday. They did not say whether more businesses were searched.

Salem police said 57-year-old Peter Leavitt of Peabody, 52-year-old Fang Liu of Peabody and 64-year-old Barry St. Onge of Shrewsbury were taken into custody and will be charged with trafficking of a person for sexual servitude, deriving support from prostitution and keeping a house of prostitution.

The Salem Police Department noted that human trafficking “involves the recruitment, harboring, transportation, provision, or obtaining of individuals for the purposes of forced labor or commercial exploitation.” It said in a press release that it “emphasizes that individuals involved in these situations are regarded as victims and are treated accordingly throughout the investigative process.”

Authorities did not say when the suspects would be arraigned or if they had attorneys.

“We see this as much a rescue operation as it is a criminal investigation,” Salem Police Chief Lucas Miller said in a statement. “The exploitation of victims of human trafficking will not be tolerated in the city of Salem.”

news_communities.title: East Boston getting popular gourmet doughnut/sandwich shop
news_communities.author: Boston Restaurant Talk
news_communities.summary:
[This story first appeared on Boston Restaurant Talk.]

The team behind a handful of gourmet doughnut shops and sandwich shops in the Boston area will be bringing the two concepts to a waterfront location.

According to a Facebook reel from Gallows Group founder Rebecca Roth Gullo, Blackbird Doughnuts and Sally’s Sandwiches will be opening in East Boston, moving into a space at Clippership Wharf. In the reel, Rebecca mentions that there will also be a “little sprinkling of The Gallows,” which was a gastropub that resided in Boston’s South End until closing in 2021, and that the business plans to open sometime in the late spring or early summer.

Locations of Blackbird Doughnuts can be found in Boston’s South End, Beacon Hill, Fenway, Brighton, Cambridge’s Harvard Square, Newton Centre, and Watertown, while Sally’s Sandwiches can be found in the South End, Harvard Square, and Brighton. The website for all locations can be found at blackbirddoughnuts.com.

news_communities.title: Chelsea High School teacher on leave after allegations of inappropriate behavior
news_communities.author: Griffin Gonzalez
news_communities.summary:
A high school teacher in Chelsea, Massachusetts, has been placed on leave after allegedly having inappropriate conversations with students.

Parents and students say the inappropriate behavior went on for years. The district took action after students staged a walkout at Chelsea High School this week.

“For the past three years, I’ve been enduring a teacher who has been inappropriate towards myself and other students,” 17-year-old Jelannie Hunter told the Chelsea School Committee this week.

“When you’re working with children, there are boundaries,” her mother told NBC10 Boston in an interview Thursday.

She said she went to the school for the past three years complaining of the teacher’s conversations with her daughter.

Students at Chelsea High School walked out of class Wednesday to protest the lack of action taken by the district despite complaints from students and parents.

“As of today, the employee involved has been placed on leave,” Chelsea Public Schools said in a letter to the Chelsea High School community Wednesday. “The school district has also initiated an independent investigation of the concerns raised regarding this matter.”

The district also said in the letter that while it “encourages and supports students’ constitutional rights to freedom of speech and expression,” it planned to discipline some students who stayed in the halls longer and “and disrupted the instructional day for themselves and others who did not participate.”

NBC10 Boston is not naming the teacher, as no charges have been filed against her.

We reached out to the district to see whether or not she would be paid throughout her leave and why action was taken now after years of complaint, but have not received a response.

news_communities.title: As Artemis II crew splashes down, Navy sailor from Mass. was set to greet them
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A U.S. Navy sailor from Massachusetts was expected to be among the first people to greet the Artemis II astronauts when they returned to Earth Friday evening.

Chief Hospital Corpsman Vlad Link is a member of the dive medical team that was set to open the Orion capsule, the U.S. Navy said in a media release. Link, who is from Chelsea, was expected to help get the astronauts out of the capsule safely after splashdown and make an initial medical assessment of their condition.

Link has 18 years of experience in dive medicine.

“I have been exposed to the Navy since I was a young teenager, and I’m proud to represent both my family and hometown,” Link said in the media release. “Contributing our efforts to NASA and the Artemis II mission is something we take great pride in as part of that legacy.”

NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Christina Koch and Victor Glover and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen returned to Earth on Friday evening after 10 days in space. This involved a fiery plunge through the atmosphere, ending in a splashdown in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of San Diego.

news_communities.title: No charges being filed against owner of dog that attacked second dog, its owner
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain and Monica Madeja
news_communities.summary:
A woman whose dog police say attacked a man and another dog in Everett, Massachusetts, will not face criminal charges in the matter, according to the department.

She did appear in court in Chelsea Friday, but on unrelated warrants, for shoplifting and larceny charges.

Everett police told NBC10 Boston there are no charges they can file in connection with the attack on Ferry Street Tuesday afternoon. In it, an off-leash dog charged a man and mauled his own, smaller dog, injuring the man as well in the process.

Surveillance video showed them, and the 49-year-old woman, who was later arrested for several outstanding warrants after officers saw her walking the dog Thursday night.

The 11-month-old hound was with animal control in quarantine on Friday.

The small dog that was attacked is recovering along with his owner, P.J. Peterson, who said he broke his collarbone and was bitten by the hound.

Peterson’s beloved 8-year-old shih-tzu, Mushroom, has massive wounds to her stomach.

“I can’t wait to hold her. I went to see her and she wants to jump in my arms and I can’t even pick her up because of my shoulder,” he said. “So it’s just like she’s laying in a kennel and she can’t move and she’s not doing anything and I walk in and she spruces up and she wants to jump in my arms and I can’t and it really sucks.”

Mushroom was set to go home with Peterson Friday night. After

“If she didn’t run off, it’d be one thing, but there was no accountability,” he said. “She took off thinking that it was not that big a deal. She couldn’t get away with it. It’s just not right.”

Peterson said he’s never had an interaction with the woman and dog before.

news_communities.title: Man convicted of killing Salem State student in 1986 sentenced to life in prison
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
A man found guilty last month in the 1986 murder of a Massachusetts college student was sentenced Thursday to life in prison without parole.

John Carey, 66, was serving a 20-year sentence for trying to kill his neighbor by choking her in 2007 when he was charged with the murder of 20-year-old Claire Gravel of North Andover.

Gravel, a student at Salem State College, was strangled with her tank top. Prosecutors with the Essex County District Attorney’s Office said a breakthrough came when testing found samples of Carey’s DNA on the garment.

During closing arguments in Essex County Superior Court, Assistant District Attorney Kim Faitella told jurors, “he forcefully grabbed Claire’s tank top and twisted it and strangled her to death to satisfy his sexual interest in strangulation.”

Gravel’s body was found in the woods in Beverly a day after she had been at a pub in Salem.

“The family of Claire Gravel has waited 40 long years for justice,” District Attorney Paul Tucker said in a statement following Carey’s conviction. “The prosecutors here today, together with our partners in law enforcement, never gave up on Claire’s case and today we are pleased that at least the family has some answers – some closure.”

Carey was previously convicted of the attempted murder of Rosemary Diskin, whom he choked inside her Hamilton home.

His defense attorney, Mark Booker, had argued that police failed to conduct a thorough investigation in 1986 and pointed to what he described as missing records and inconsistencies in witness statements over time.

news_communities.title: Woman arrested after dog injures another dog, its owner, in Everett, police say
news_communities.author: Amelia Fabiano
news_communities.summary:
A man and his dog were violently attacked by another dog in Everett, Massachusetts, on Tuesday afternoon, and police said Thursday night that a woman had been arrested.

PJ Peterson told NBC10 Boston he was walking his 8-year-old Shih Tzu, Mushroom, along Ferry Street Tuesday afternoon when a woman’s pit bull charged toward them.

Peterson said the woman yelled for him to pick Mushroom up, but before he could react, the other dog knocked him to the ground, breaking his collarbone.

“She knew her dog was going to attack mine,” he said.

The pit bull then grabbed Mushroom in its mouth, causing severe injuries, he explained.

“I thought my dog was dead,” Peterson said. “I was holding her guts together.”

Peterson said he was able to free his dog, but the other owner and her dog took off and never came back.

“She said, ‘I’m going to tie up my dog,’ and she never looked back,” he said. “She was gone from that point on.”

Surveillance video from a nearby convenience store showed a woman walking with a dog away from the area shortly after the attack.

Everett police said Thursday night that 49-year-old Andrea Bono of Everett had been taken into custody on several outstanding warrants.

The animal, which police described as an 11-month-old hound, was taken in by Everett Animal Control and is being placed in quarantine, police said.

Peterson said Mushroom’s injuries racked up about $17,000 in veterinary bills, but she’s expected to come home Friday.

“Anybody who knows me, knows my dog’s just everything to me.”

He said he’ll also be out of work for at least a month while recovering from his broken bone.

Watch more of the latest big news stories in Boston:

Peterson said he’d seen this woman and her dog around the neighborhood before, but never had prior issues with either.

“Please turn yourself in,” he said before police announced an arrest. “You know what you did, you know what your dog did, you just got to take accountability for it.”

Everett police and fire were called to the scene after the attack and transported Peterson and Mushroom for treatment.

Bono is set to be arraigned Friday in Malden District Court. It was not immediately clear if she had an attorney.

news_communities.title: WILD PHOTOS: Truck crashes through Eastern Bank building in Everett
news_communities.author: Marc Fortier and Mark Garfinkel
news_communities.summary:
A truck crashed right through an Eastern Bank branch in Everett, Massachusetts, on Thursday morning, leaving a large hole in the building.

The crash occurred sometime after 7 a.m. at an Eastern Bank branch at 1763 Revere Beach Parkway.

Photos from the scene showed a truck that appeared have gone completely through a building, with a large field of debris strewn all over the exterior. A large hole could be seen at the front of the building, right underneath the Eastern Bank logo.

Everett police and firefighters were at the scene. Yellow police tape surrounds the building.

Everett Police Chief Paul Strong said it appears the driver had some type of a medical episode and crashed through the bank.

Both occupants of the truck were taken to a local hospital with minor injuries.

Strong said the building sustained major damage and the building inspector is on scene.

news_communities.title: Saugus homeowners demand answers about sky-high water bills
news_communities.author: Mary Markos
news_communities.summary:
Saugus residents say they’re being charged thousands of dollars for water they never used — and they’re running out of patience as they wait for the town to explain what’s going on.

Homeowners across multiple neighborhoods report receiving water bills far higher than anything they’ve seen before — some of them larger than a monthly mortgage payment and, in at least two cases, larger than a yearly tax bill.

‘My last bill was $6,800’

Debra Fauci says she opened her most recent bill to find a charge of $6,800. Weeks later, another arrived — this one for $5,300.

“It’s over $12,000,” Fauci said. “It’s double, triple a mortgage payment.”

Fauci says she calculated what the town said she used: About 500 gallons a day — which she says is the equivalent of filling “13 bathtubs filled with water” every day.

She paid the town $1,000 toward the first bill. What she didn’t expect, she says, was the town placing a lien on her condo and withdrawing the remainder directly from her mortgage escrow account — a move she didn’t learn about until her mortgage company called.

The withdrawal left her escrow negative, forcing her monthly mortgage to increase to make up the difference.

“Give me my money back. Give me some type of adjustment in the billing,” she said.

No leaks — and an unanswered test request

Fauci recently had her old meter replaced as part of a town initiative. The device comes with an app capable of showing usage by the hour and detecting leaks. For the month she’s had it, she says the app has shown no leaks detected — and still isn’t registering any new usage at all.

Fauci requested her old meter be sent out for third-party testing. She says it’s been a month without any update from the town.

A Select Board member is experiencing the same thing

Saugus Select Board member Anthony Cogliano says his own water bills have skyrocketed — and he’s just as baffled.

“My water bill was $1,900. I went to the DPW, checked everything, had toilets tested, no leaks,” he said. “This time, my bill was $3,500.”

He says he’ll be billed about $7,000 for the year.

“That’s insane,” Cogliano said. “There’s no way in hell my water bill is $7,000 for the year — but it’s going to prove to be that way.”

Like Fauci, Cogliano says plumbers inspected his home and found no leaks or abnormal usage.

“Something’s not right,” he said.

Residents say this isn’t the first time

Former Town Meeting Member Bill Brown fought a similar battle five years ago. He received a $1,075 water bill with just three people in his household. After weeks of complaints, the town reviewed his case and determined his meter was broken. The bill was cut in half.

“When you get a bill that’s outrageous, you’ve got to have answers,” Brown said. “People want answers on what’s going on.”

Meters in Saugus are being replaced — but residents want transparency

The town has been in the process of replacing water meters across Saugus. Officials have said that new meters can record usage more accurately — sometimes resulting in higher bills for households that previously had aging or under measuring meters.

But residents like Fauci and Cogliano say the numbers they’re seeing simply don’t line up with their habits, inspections or the readings on the new smart meter apps.

“We need answers,” Fauci said. “Because people can’t afford this. I certainly can’t.”

Town response

NBC10 Boston reached out to the town manager, the treasurer and members of the Saugus Select Board. As of publication, Cogliano is the only one who responded.

There is precedent for the town reducing or eliminating excessively high water bills — but for residents now facing thousands of dollars in charges, they say they need clarity soon.

news_communities.title: Teen held without bail on murder charge in Chelsea stabbing
news_communities.author: Mike Pescaro, Marc Fortier, Griffin Gonzalez and Sebastian Agudelo Gomez
news_communities.summary:
A teenager has been charged with murder in connection with a deadly stabbing on Thursday night in Chelsea, Massachusetts.

Sergio Josué Castellanos, 18, was held without bail at his arraignment Friday. He was allowed to hide his face in court.

Police responded to the intersection of Chestnut Street and Everett Avenue around 6:45 p.m. Thursday, and said multiple people were injured in stabbings.

Authorities said Friday that two people were injured: Castellanos, who suffered minor injuries, and 25-year-old Eduardo Rózales López of Chelsea.

Prosecutors with the Suffolk County District Attorney’s Office said López was treated at the scene for stab wounds to his neck and torso. He was transported to Massachusetts General Hospital, where he died from his injuries early Friday morning.

Reviewing video, police described seeing five people chasing after López. A man identified as Castellanos could be seen striking López in a stabbing motion while he’s on the ground, prosecutors said.

The video allegedly showed Castellanos and three other people fleeing. Police found him and three other males behind the fence of a home nearby on Chestnut Street.

Castellanos, who was tending to a stab wound to his leg, had changed his clothes from those seen on camera, which were found next to him, prosecutors said. He was transported to an area hospital for treatment.

Authorities said a black machete with stains on the blade was recovered.

Castellanos’ defense attorney, asking that his client be released on bail with GPS monitoring, raised questions about the identity of the attacker. He noted that in the video in which prosecutors described Castellanos making a stabbing motion, no knife was seen.

The judge ordered Castellanos held without bail.

Family members told Telemundo Nueva Inglaterra that López arrived from Guatemala seven years ago and leaves behind a 2-year-old child.

People in the area were shocked by the confrontation.

“It was kind of scary, I’m not going to lie. You still see the remnants of blood on the floor,” neighbor Shauniece White said Thursday. “It’s extremely concerning. As you see, it’s only been one nice day so far. If it’s already happening now, I can only imagine what the summer has in store for us. I’m hopeful that the police will come out and do due diligence to keep the community safe, but wishful thinking in a time like this.”

Massachusetts State Police are assisting Chelsea police with the investigation.

news_communities.title: Multiple people hospitalized after being stabbed in Chelsea
news_communities.author: Griffin Gonzalez and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
Police in Chelsea, Massachusetts, said multiple people were injured in stabbings Thursday evening.

Officers responded to the intersection of Chestnut Street and Everett Avenue around 6:45 p.m.

While the Chelsea Police Department did not say how many people were stabbed, it confirmed all those hurt were hospitalized, including one person who is in serious condition with potentially life-threatening injuries.

Authorities did not say whether anyone had been arrested.

“It was kind of scary, I’m not going to lie. You still see the remnants of blood on the floor,” said neighbor Shauniece White. “It’s extremely concerning. As you see, it’s only been one nice day so far. If it’s already happening now, I can only imagine what the summer has in store for us. I’m hopeful that the police will come out and do due diligence to keep the community safe, but wishful thinking in a time like this.”

Massachusetts State Police are investigating alongside the city’s department.

“Chelsea Detectives and Mass State Police Detectives are currently working this very serious incident and we are ready to utilize any and all of our resources to give our community the peace of mind our citizens deserve,” Chelsea Police Chief Keith Houghton said in a statement.

Anyone with information is asked to call 617-466-4800. Anonymous tips can also be left by calling 617-466-4880.

news_communities.title: ‘Horrific tragedy': New details in case of high school student charged with murdering Danvers woman
news_communities.author: Kaitlin McKinley Becker, Munashe Kwangwari and Marc Fortier
news_communities.summary:
An 18-year-old from Lynn, Massachusetts, was ordered held without bail on Friday after he allegedly murdered a woman insider her Danvers home in what authorities believe was a random attack.

The Essex County District Attorney’s Office announced Thursday night that Anthony DeMayo, a senior at Bishop Fenwick High School in Peabody, was being charged with murder in connection to the death of 68-year-old Janet Swallow.

DeMayo was arraigned Friday afternoon in Salem District Court. He pleaded not guilty and was ordered held without bail. He’ll be sent to Bridgewater State Hospital for a mental evaluation and is scheduled to return to court next month.

The case was called briefly just before 10:30 a.m., but the judge ordered DeMayo to be evaluated first to determine his competency.

When DeMayo returned to court for his arraignment later in the day, court psychologist Joyce Perotta told the judge she had interviewed DeMayo for about an hour with his lawyer present. She said he presented with depressive symptoms, including restricted affect, anhedonia and suicidal ideation, and it appears those symptoms have existed for some time.

Perotta said DeMayo demonstrated “adequate understanding” of the court proceedings but she had concerns about his rational understanding and the potential impact of his current mental state on his legal decision making.

She recommended sending him to Bridgewater State Hospital under tight security for further assessment.

The judge agreed with Perotta’s assessment and ordered DeMayo to be held without bail and sent to Bridgewater State pending a pretrial hearing on April 1. His attorney did not object to the order.

The investigation that led to DeMayo’s arrest began after a 911 caller told police that a man was acting erratically, walking around on Standish Road in Lynn with what looked like a blood-stained knife. Officers responded and found DeMayo, taking him to Salem Hospital.

DeMayor reportedly told Lynn police that he had killed a woman the night before in Danvers.

A search of DeMayo’s Lynn home revealed blood-stained clothing, and along with data from his cell phone, investigators contacted the Danvers Police Department, who conducted a well-being check and found Swallow dead inside her home at 17 Amherst St.

Prosecutors said Swallow was found dead, alone, “suffering wounds consistent with a homicide.” They said DeMayo apparently entered the home by climbing through a window and stabbed Swallow while she was in bed. They said no items were found to be missing from the home and they believe the attack was random.

The longtime Danvers resident was an ICU nurse at Lahey Hospital in Burlington and leaves behind two sons.

Scott James, Lahey’s chief nursing officer, released a statement Friday calling Swallow “a beloved and valued member of the Lahey Hospital & Medical Center community” who will be “greatly missed.”

“We share our deepest condolences with her family and loved ones and her colleagues in the Nursing Department and across our hospital. Our thoughts are with them during this very difficult time,” he added.

Officials said there is no known connection between DeMayo and Swallow. No other details about what happened have been shared by authorities.

Bishop Fenwick High School President Tom Nunan Jr. issued a statement Friday morning about the murder:

“We are aware of the investigation, and the information released regarding that investigation, by the Danvers Police Department and the Essex County District Attorney’s Office.

We know this matter involves serious charges against an 18-year-old student who is in the custody and care of the proper authorities.

The reports reveal the incident took place off campus, did not involve any other members of our school, did not seem to indicate any connection between the victim and the person being charged with the crime, and does not present any threat to the Bishop Fenwick community.

We are cooperating fully with local law enforcement in this investigation, which is ongoing. Because this is an active criminal investigation, we defer to the appropriate legal authorities to provide any additional information. We will not have further comment on the matter.

Our thoughts and prayers are with the victim, her family, and all who have been affected by this horrific tragedy.

As a community of faith and hope, our focus is on our faculty and staff, our families, and especially our students. We are here to help them, to provide for them the supports they need, and to be present for them in this terribly difficult moment.  To that end, additional counselors and other trained professionals will be on campus today and Monday.

Thank you for your understanding and your prayer.“

Danvers Police Chief Jamie Lovell also issued a statement expressing his department’s “deepest condolences” to the Swallow family, her friends and everyone who knew her.

“The loss is being felt across our town, and many are struggling to make sense of it,” he said.

news_communities.title: Danvers murder suspect was spotted wandering around Lynn before his arrest
news_communities.author: Amelia Fabiano
news_communities.summary:
A young man raised alarm on Thursday as he wandered streets and backyards in Lynn, Massachusetts, carrying a knife. Neighbors who saw him were shocked to learn that he’s accused in a murder in Danvers.

Multiple people told NBC10 Boston that they saw 18-year-old Anthony DeMayo walking around at about noon Thursday with a large knife in his hand. Later, he would be identified as the suspect in the murder of Janet Swallow in Danvers.

Several of them called 911, which led police to arrest him in a backyard on Standish Road. The whole thing was caught on home surveillance video.

“They said they saw a crazy guy with a knife, and I’m like, ‘How did this happen,’ you know? It’s scary, you never think, where you live, it’s going to happen,” John Bossi said.

Security cameras captured the moments leading up to the arrest. It starts with a vehicle crashing into Nells Pond in Lynn around 11:40 a.m.

“It’s hard to even get there. I don’t know how he got through. There’s no easy way for a car to get there,” Bossi said.

About 13 minutes later, DeMayo is seen walking down Bow Ridge Road in a bright green shirt, swinging a knife around in his right hand.

Then, he goes to Nells Pond Road. Ashley O’Brien was outside with her ducks when she saw him walk by.

“I thought for sure that this guy was obviously mentally unwell. I didn’t want to make a scene and run away or anything like that, cause he made eye contact with me,” she said.

She immediately called 911, the first of several calls from the neighborhood.

“I just waited till he passed my house, went in, told my husband and called the cops,” O’Brien said. “When we came back out, he was just kind of stabbing all the bushes outside and running a knife across the houses and stuff like that — it was very bizarre.”

Rhonda Deparolesa was another on nearby Standish Road.

“I was sitting on my chair, in the recliner, and all of the sudden, I see somebody jump over my fence,” Deparolesa said. “He just laid flat on his back on the bulkhead and just wasn’t moving, so my husband was upstairs, and I said, ‘Something’s going on here.'”

Deparolesa said he tried to open her door at one point, but thankfully, it was locked.

“What if he did get in my house? My husband and I might not be here,” she said. “You know, you think you’re safe in your house. Sometimes you’re not.”

By around 12:30 p.m., police were after him. They found him in a backyard a few doors down from Deparolesa’s home. He was arrested and placed in a police cruiser.

“I’m just absolutely shocked that a young kid, 18 years old, had his whole life ahead of him, and had to go and do this,” Deparolesa said. “For what? Why?”

Everyone who spoke to NBC10 Boston was shocked to learn DeMayo is accused of murder.

“I just thought this guy needed some help and I just called. I did not think that he’d just murdered somebody,” O’Brien said.

Police confirmed DeMayo was a student at Bishop Fenwick High School in Peabody. They did not comment on the investigation, but said they are fully cooperating with local law enforcement.

Their thoughts and prayers are on the victim, her family, and everyone impacted by the horrific tragedy. They said their focus right now is on staff and students.

They had additional counselors on campus Friday and will on Monday, as well.

news_communities.title: Lynn teen charged with murder of woman in Danvers home
news_communities.author: Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
3/13/26 UPDATE: Bishop Fenwick High School has released a statement on this “horrific tragedy” involving senior Anthony DeMayo, who is due in court Friday.

A teenager from Lynn, Massachusetts, is charged with the murder of a woman in her Danvers home.

Essex County District Attorney Paul Tucker said the investigation began in Lynn Thursday afternoon when a man was seen behaving erratically, walking on Standish Road with a knife with an apparent blood stain. He was taken to Salem Hospital.

Police responded to his home in Lynn. Evidence uncovered at the home and new information received led investigators to the home of 68-year-old Janet Swallow on Amherst Street in Danvers.

Swallow was found dead in the home, Tucker said.

The suspect, identified as 18-year-old Anthony DeMayo, was charged with murder.

No connection between DeMayo — a senior at Bishop Fenwick High School in Peabody — and Swallow has been found. Authorities believe the incident to be random in nature.

DeMayo is due to be arraigned Friday in Salem District Court, at which point authorities said more information would be available.

It was not immediately clear if DeMayo has an attorney.

news_communities.title: Falling bricks from Chelsea triple decker left vacant after fire
news_communities.author: Matt Fortin
news_communities.summary:
Bricks fell onto the sidewalk off a vacant building in Chelsea, Massachusetts, and emergency crews have created a collapse zone in the area as a precaution.

The Chelsea Fire Department said that it responded to the 120 block of Washington Avenue on Monday night for a report of bricks falling from a building. It was a triple decker that had been vacant since a serious fire there in January.

First responders originally heard that a pedestrian may have been hit by a falling brick, but they didn’t find anyone on scene.

Crews said a section of exterior brick wall over the second floor window collapsed onto the sidewalk below.

The city’s inspectional services determined the building was unsafe and created a collapse zone in the area until the owner can fix up the building.

Fire officials said the sidewalk and a section of Washington Avenue is closed between Orange Street and Addison Street. Washington Ave. is open to vehicle traffic so the MBTA 111 bus isn’t impacted, officials added.

news_communities.title: Man dead after fire in Salem, Mass.
news_communities.author: Marc Fortier, Thea DiGiammerino and Jericho Tran
news_communities.summary:
A man has died after a fire in Salem, Massachusetts, on Tuesday.

Salem Fire Chief Alan Dionne said they were called to 18 Roslyn St. around 1:13 a.m. Firefighters pulled one person from a third-floor unit. That 62-year-old man was taken to Mass General Brigham in Salem, where he died of his injuries.

No other injuries were reported.

Dionne said there were hoarding conditions in the home that made a rescue more challenging for firefighters.

“It was incredibly difficult to find the victim, there was so much stuff in the building,” Dionne said.

John Payne, a former deputy chief with the Salem Fire Department, suited up and ran straight into the smoke-filled home just six weeks into his retirement.

“When I got to the third floor is where I started feeling a little bit of heat coming in because the door was wide open,” Payne said. “I had an old jacket of mine, so I grabbed that and a flashlight, and I went back up with one of the detectives, and we had an extinguisher, and I said ‘You ready?'”

Joined by a police detective, he ran in and attempted to save the man trapped on the third floor.

“Shirt over his face, we took one step in, and I go, ‘Damn, we’re not getting in here,'” Payne said.

Firefighters soon responded and replaced the duo, but were unable to save the man.

Dionne described their actions as “incredibly dangerous but obviously heroic.”

Aerial images showed several fire vehicles outside of a multi-story building, with a ladder extended to the roof.

The cause of the fire remains under investigation.

news_communities.title: Man sentenced to life in prison for 2023 killing of girlfriend in Chelsea
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino and Staff Reports
news_communities.summary:
A man who pleaded guilty to stabbing his estranged girlfriend to death at her apartment in Chelsea, Massachusetts, in 2023 was sentenced to life in prison on Monday.

Angel Alvarez, 68, was charged with killing 59-year-old Margarita Morehead in May 2023. He was arrested hours after Morehead was found fatally stabbed on the floor of her apartment on LaFayette Avenue on May 8.

The pair had dated for several years, and Alvarez had lived on and off in Morehead’s home. At the time of the killing, Morehead had a restraining order out against Alvarez. Prosecutors said that Alvarez was frequently jealous of her and hostile, accusing her of cheating on him.

Alvarez pleaded guilty to second-degree murder, assault and battery with a dangerous weapon and violation of protection order on February 11. He was sentenced to life in prison with the possibility of parole after 17 years.

If you or someone you know is experiencing domestic violence, contact the National Domestic Violence Hotline by calling 1-800-799-SAFE (7233), visiting www.thehotline.org or texting LOVEIS to 22522.

On the morning of her death, Alvarez was at Morehead’s apartment and they were arguing over her attempt to leave him, prosecutors said.

Prosecutors said Morehead punched Alvarez that day and when she told him her daughter would see the injuries, he became enraged. Alvarez then grabbed a kitchen knife and stabbed Morehead multiple times in the chest, cutting off two of his own fingers. He then left, leaving Morehead to bleed out on the floor.

A neighbor heard Morehead’s screams and saw a man, later identified as Alvarez, walk out, leaving a trail of blood, but he didn’t call for help, prosecutors said. Instead, after the better part of an hour, his partner called a domestic violence hotline, and a staff member there called 911.

Morehead was found unresponsive, with the kitchen knife near her. Prosecutors said Alvarez bought new clothes at a Target, where his bloody old clothes were found, and told others that he had stabbed Morehead and was thinking about harming himself. He turned himself in that evening, allegedly at his family’s advice.

Morehead was a mother of four. Her children spoke during the sentencing hearing.

Our mother, Margarita, was not defined by the way her life ended, but by the way she lived, by the love she gave to family, the guidance she offered to others, and the quiet strength she carried every day,” one of her sons told the court.

“Domestic violence didn’t just take my mother’s life. It took a grandmother from my son, a parent from me, and a piece of our family that can never be replaced,” her daughter added.

news_communities.title: Man convicted of killing Salem State student in 1986
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
A man was found guilty Tuesday in the 1986 murder of a Massachusetts college student.

John Carey, 66, was serving a 20-year sentence for trying to kill his neighbor by choking her in 2007 when he was charged with the murder of 20-year-old Claire Gravel of North Andover.

Gravel, a student at Salem State College, was strangled with her tank top. Prosecutors with the Essex County District Attorney’s Office said a breakthrough came when testing found samples of Carey’s DNA on the garment.

During closing arguments in Essex County Superior Court Monday, Assistant District Attorney Kim Faitella told jurors, “he forcefully grabbed Claire’s tank top and twisted it and strangled her to death to satisfy his sexual interest in strangulation.”

Gravel’s body was found in the woods in Beverly a day after she had been at a pub in Salem.

“The family of Claire Gravel has waited 40 long years for justice,” District Attorney Paul Tucker said in a statement Tuesday. “The prosecutors here today, together with our partners in law enforcement, never gave up on Claire’s case and today we are pleased that at least the family has some answers – some closure.”

Carey was previously convicted of the attempted murder of Rosemary Diskin, whom he choked inside her Hamilton home.

His defense attorney, Mark Booker, said in court Monday that jurors “should find Mr. Carey not guilty,” arguing that police failed to conduct a thorough investigation in 1986 and pointing to what he described as missing records and inconsistencies in witness statements over time.

Carey is due to be sentenced on March 26.

news_communities.title: Case against man charged in 1986 murder of Salem student goes to jury
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain
news_communities.summary:
Update March 3: John Carey has been convicted in the murder of Claire Gravel. Read the latest here.

A Massachusetts jury began deliberations Monday in the decades-old killing of a Salem State College student, weighing whether a man — already serving time for a separate strangulation attack — is responsible for her death.

Prosecutors with the Essex County District Attorney’s Office allege that John Carey, 66, murdered 20-year-old Claire Gravel of North Andover in 1986. Gravel’s body was found in the woods in Beverly a day after she had been at a pub in neighboring Salem. Witnesses said she was last seen being dropped off at her apartment in Salem the previous night.

During closing arguments in Essex County Superior Court, Assistant District Attorney Kim Faitella told jurors, “he forcefully grabbed Claire’s tank top and twisted it and strangled her to death to satisfy his sexual interest in strangulation.”

Carey is already serving a 20-year sentence for attempting to kill a neighbor Rosemary Diskin in Hamilton in 2007 by choking her inside her home.

Defense attorney Mark Booker urged jurors to reject the prosecution’s case, telling them, “You should find Mr. Carey not guilty.”

Booker argued investigators failed to conduct a thorough investigation in 1986 and pointed to what he described as missing records and inconsistencies in witness statements over time.

Despite the presence of DNA evidence, legal analysts say the case is not necessarily straightforward. The defense must only raise reasonable doubt, particularly in a case built in part on decades-old witness testimony.

“All the defendant has to do is create that reasonable doubt,” said NBC10 Boston legal analyst Michael Coyne. “And when you’re dealing with witnesses, memories and statements over such a long period of time, there will be differences between what the trial testimony is and what it was at one point, what their witness statement said.”

Jurors are expected to continue deliberating until they reach a verdict.

news_communities.title: New details revealed in court about Malden teacher accused of raping high school student
news_communities.author: Kaitlin McKinley Becker
news_communities.summary:
A 54-year-old teacher accused of raping a high school student in Malden, Massachusetts, faced a judge Thursday, a day after his arrest.

Authorities allege Scott Marino gave alcohol to an 18-year-old who is known to him and then sexually assaulted her last Wednesday, Feb. 18, at his Stoneham home, where the victim was currently living. Marino was arrested a week after the alleged incident and has been charged with two counts of rape, one count of indecent assault and battery and one count of furnishing alcohol to a person under the age of 21, according to the Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office.

Prosecutors provided shocking new details Thursday in Woburn District Court at the arraignment for Marino, who teaches at Linden STEAM Academy in Malden and coaches the girls’ basketball team at Malden High School.

Resources for victims of sexual assault are available through the National Sexual Violence Resources Center and the National Sexual Assault Telephone Hotline at 800-656-4673, and Massachusetts provides this list of statewide and resources for sexual assault survivors.

Jacob McCrindle of the Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office said in court that the alleged victim in this case had been in the foster care of Marino and his wife at the time this occurred. The victim, who moved in with the Marinos at their Stoneham home in the fall, was a basketball player for Marino during the season, and the two were known to each other for many years.

According to prosecutors, Marino’s wife was scheduled to take a trip out of state last Wednesday. The victim was on school vacation, so she went with Marino and a grandfather who lives in a basement apartment at the home to an Ash Wednesday mass. They dropped the older man off at home, and then Marino and the victim went driving because she had a learner’s permit and was learning to drive on the highway. They made the decision to go into Boston to get a takeout seafood dinner, and on the way home, Marino had them stop at a liquor store where surveillance video shows he purchased a strawberry margarita mix, a large bottle of Patron tequila and some other agave liquor.

The two then went back to Marino’s Stoneham home and he allegedly gave her a large margarita. She drank some of it but didn’t like it, though Marino allegedly encouraged her to keep drinking. He later poured them shots of tequila, and they ate the seafood dinner they got. The victim later told police that she was feeling ill so she went to her bedroom where she vomited many times, eventually lying down in bed.

Marino then came in and sexually assaulted her two times, prosecutors said in court. The victim later retrieved her cell phone and began texting friends and ultimately an aunt, begging for help.

McCrindle said that the aunt called 911 as she was driving to the Marino’s home, and the victim was taken to Winchester Hospital for a full sexual assault examination.

“This is a relationship where the defendant was the victim’s foster parent, he took her into his home, that’s a position of trust, as well as coupled with the fact that the defendant is a teacher, or was a teacher at the Malden Public Schools and he was also her coach for many years,” McCrindle said in court, arguing for $100,000 cash bail. “The defendant used I would suggest that relationship, he took advantage of that relationship, there’s a significant age difference, the defendant is 54 years old, the victim is 18 years old. There’s also a significant size difference that’s described in the police report between the defendant and the alleged victim…. it’s also noteworthy that the defendant appears to have waited until his wife left the home so he was more isolated with the victim…clearly some planning went into this.”

Marino’s defense attorney argued before the judge that he’s a respected educator with deep community support who has “never been in court in his life.”

The judge ultimately ordered $75,000 cash bail based on the gravity of circumstances along with several conditions requested by the commonwealth, including that Marino stay away and have no contact from the victim and any witnesses, stay away from all Malden school property and events, be fitted with a GPS bracelet to enforce exclusive zones including the victim’s home address, her school, her workplace and a gym that she frequents, surrender his passport and not be allowed to leave the state.

The judge also added the condition that Marino be alcohol free and submit to SCRAM testing.

Resources for victims of sexual assault are available through the National Sexual Violence Resources Center and the National Sexual Assault Telephone Hotline at 800-656-4673, and Massachusetts provides this list of statewide and resources for sexual assault survivors.

Malden Superintendent Timothy Sippel previously said the district was informed Wednesday that Marino had been taken into custody in connection with “a serious incident alleged to have occurred outside of working hours and involving one of our high school students.”

Marino was immediately placed on leave, the superintendent added, noting that the district is cooperating fully with law enforcement agencies in their investigation.

“I understand how upsetting and distressing this is to our community given the serious nature of these allegations,” Sippel said in a statement. “The safety of our students and staff is our highest priority.”

A plea of not guilty was entered on Marino’s behalf. He is due back in court for a probable cause hearing on April 16.

news_communities.title: Malden teacher and coach charged with rape of teen
news_communities.author: Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
A teacher and coach in Malden, Massachusetts, has charged with the rape of an 18-year-old.

Malden Public Schools said Scott Marino had been arrested over a “serious incident” involving a high school student. The Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office and Stoneham Police Department later said he had been charged with two counts of rape, one count of indecent assault and battery and one count of furnishing alcohol to a person under 21.

Marino is accused of providing alcohol to the victim, who is known to him and sexually assaulting her on Feb. 18, prosecutors said.

Superintendent Timothy Sippel said Marino teaches at Linden STEAM Academy — which serves kindergarten through eighth grade — and is the head coach of the Malden High School girls’ basketball team.

Sippel said that Marino’s “arrest is in connection with a serious incident alleged to have occurred outside of working hours and involving one of our high school students.”

“I understand how upsetting and distressing this is to our community given the serious nature of these allegations,” the superintendent said.

Marino is due to be arraigned Thursday in Woburn District Court. It was not immediately clear if he had an attorney.

news_communities.title: Brookline police report outlines murder of MIT professor
news_communities.author: Glenn Jones
news_communities.summary:
Two months after the murder of an MIT physics professor outside his home in Brookline, Massachusetts, a police report reveals the victim’s daughter almost came face-to-face with her father’s killer.

Nuno Loureiro’s daughter told investigators she went toward the door after hearing the doorbell ring, but her dad called her back so he could answer the door instead. When he opened it, he was shot multiple times.

Six gun casings were recovered by investigators in the foyer.

The couple and two of their three daughters were home at the time of the shooting.

Recently, Ines Loureiro, the physics professor’s widow, returned to a dance class, according to Dr. Eurydice Hirsey, a classmate.

“You have to work very hard after a shock like that to put the pieces of your life together,” Hirsey said. “And having children at home, there’s no excuse. You have to.”

The family is originally from Portugal. They continue to live in Brookline.

Investigators have not given a clear motive for the crime. They say the killer was a former classmate and fellow physicist from Portugal and was responsible for the deadly mass shooting at Brown University — where he once studied before dropping out — two days earlier.

Two students — Ella Cook and MukhammadAziz Umurzokov — were killed and nine others were injured.

The gunman then fled to New Hampshire, where he fatally turned the gun on himself, federal prosecutors said.

news_communities.title: Fire breaks out at Lynn home
news_communities.author: Jericho Tran
news_communities.summary:
Crews are battling a fire Monday afternoon in Lynn, Massachusetts.

The fire broke out on Burrill Avenue, the Lynn Fire Department said around 4:30 p.m. Aerial footage showed flames shooting from the roof of the building, along with heavy smoke.

About half an hour later, officials said the fire had “possession of the third floor.” Firefighters were ordered out of the building.

A short time later, the roof appeared to have burned out.

No injuries were reported. The American Red Cross of Massachusetts has been called to assist 14 people who were displaced.

“When our companies got here, they saw heavy black smoke, so they were going to try to hit it from the outside and realized that the fire had gotten inside the house, so they hit it quickly, then went inside,” Lynn Fire Chief Dan Sullivan. “By the time they got inside, it had already burned through, and then went up into the third floor.”

As of around 6:30 p.m., the heavy fire had been knocked down, fire officials said.

“My sister, her four kids and her husband were on the first floor. They saw the smoke and then they ran out,” said Mateo Velez.

His family quickly escaped the home after the fire broke out.

“My niece, she’s 5 months old, so that was the most important thing, that her and my other nephews were out,” said Velez.

Valez used to live at the home with his family.

“I saw through the back end, my old room was gone,” he said.

news_communities.title: Coletta Zapata Named Chair of Government Operations and City Council Vice President
news_communities.author: Times Staff
news_communities.summary:
Special to the Times-Free Press

Councilor Gabriela Coletta Zapata has been named as the Chair of the Government Operations Committee and City Council Vice President for the 2026-2027 legislative year. In the last council session, Coletta Zapata served as Chair of the Government Operations and Environmental Justice, Resiliency, and Parks Committee.

“I’m honored to continue serving as Chair of the Government Operations Committee and proud of what we accomplished last legislative cycle advancing responsible governance and promoting equity, sustainability, and livability across Boston,” said Coletta Zapata. “Residents entrusted us with moving the needle on the issues that impact their daily lives, and it is our responsibility not just to identify challenges, but to propose and advance real solutions that serve our communities. I look forward to collaborating with my colleagues to ensure a productive legislative session while advancing policies that make Boston a city that works for all its residents.”

As Chair of the Committee on Government Operations, she plays a central role in the City Council’s legislative process overseeing the review, analysis, and negotiation of all ordinances, home rule petitions, and special laws before they advance to the full Council or the Mayor’s desk. This position serves as the final checkpoint for accountability, legal precision, and policy alignment across all city departments, ensuring that every law enacted reflects both community needs and sound governance.

Under her leadership in the previous Council session, the City Council passed several critical ordinances, including codifying the Office of Food Justice, prioritizing surplus municipal property for affordable housing, and protecting workers from heat-related illness and injury. As Chair of the Government Operations Committee, Coletta Zapata reviewed and passed 42 Home Rule Petitions and 16 ordinances. As Chair of Environmental Justice, Resiliency, and Parks, the Council also advanced efforts to expand food access, support food pantries, and approved grants that unlock investments to strengthen and expand Boston’s open and green spaces. Her prior service as Chair of these committees and her track record of building consensus across complex policy areas position Councilor Coletta Zapata to be an effective partner to Council President Liz Breadon, helping guide the Council’s legislative priorities and ensure smooth, collaborative Council operations in her new role as Vice President.

The Boston City Council meets on Wednesdays at 12pm in the Iannella Chamber of Boston City Hall. The first Council Meeting of 2026 is scheduled for January 28, 2026. City Council meetings and hearings can be found at https://www.boston.gov/departments/city-council

For additional information please contact the Office of Councilor Gabriela Coletta by phone at (617) 635-3200 or by email at gabriela.ramirez@boston.gov.

news_communities.title: Rep. Madaro Nominates Slush King for Massachusetts Manufacturing Award – 2025
news_communities.author: Times Staff
news_communities.summary:
Special to the Times-Free Press

On Friday, January 16, Representative Adrian Madaro met with Slush King owner Mark Nolan and his daughter, Natalie Nolan, at the business’s popular Bennington Street location. Although Slush King is closed for the season, Rep. Madaro was able to tour where Slush King’s slush and other frozen treats are made in-house before presenting the business with a citation recognizing it as East Boston’s recipient of the 2025 Massachusetts Manufacturing Award.

Rep. Madaro was proud to nominate Slush King for the Massachusetts Manufacturing Award in fall 2025. While many residents are familiar with their popular walk-up window, where lines can stretch down the block on a warm summer evening, not everyone knows that Slush King manufactures many of their signature offerings themselves. The result is some of the finest Italian-style slush in the city of Boston, as well as a wide selection of gelato, ice cream, and other frozen desserts.

The Massachusetts Manufacturing Awards are hosted by the Legislative Manufacturing Caucus. Formed in August 2014, the Manufacturing Caucus includes more than 70 legislators from around the Commonwealth.  The Massachusetts Manufacturing Awards seeks to recognize and promote outstanding manufacturing businesses from across the Commonwealth.

The 10th Annual Manufacturing Awards Ceremony took place on Tuesday, October 21, 2025, at Gillette Stadium in Foxborough.

“There are few pleasures that match getting a cold treat from Slush King on a hot summer’s day in East Boston,” said Representative Madaro. “I’m proud to honor Slush King and owner Mark Nolan for their incredible, delicious tradition of authentic, locally made slush and the legacy of the Nolan family across generations of this business in East Boston. I’m already looking forward to them opening back up this spring!”

Upon accepting the citation from Madaro, Nolan said, “We’re honored to receive this award. It means a lot to us. We thank our customers for coming to our business and being so loyal to us. And we’ll see you on May 1.”

news_communities.title: Kaori Cafe Holds Grand Opening Ribbon Cutting
news_communities.author: Times Staff
news_communities.summary:
The East Boston Chamber of Commerce proudly joined the newly opened Kaori Cafe for its Grand Opening Ribbon Cutting and Coffee Connect event on Friday, January 23, at its location at 303 Meridian Street in East Boston.

Kaori Cafe provided a beautiful setting for the celebration featuring excellent coffee and delicious breakfast offerings. The well-attended ribbon cutting and Coffee Connect event offered a warm and welcoming start to the day bringing together community members to network and celebrate the newest addition to the neighborhood.

Kaori Cafe and owner Bryan Melendez were presented with citations from state and local representatives in recognition of the opening and as a welcome to the East Boston business community. The Chamber also officially welcomed Kaori Cafe as its newest member and wished the business great success in its new venture.

         ————–

Comunicado de Prensa:

La Cámara de Comercio de East Boston se unió a Kaori Cafe para su Gran Inauguración, Corte de Cinta y evento Coffee Connect

La Cámara de Comercio de East Boston se unió con orgullo al recién inaugurado Kaori Cafe para su evento de Gran Inauguración, Corte de Cinta y Coffee Connect el viernes 23 de enero de 2026, en su ubicación en el 303 de Meridian Street en East Boston.

Kaori Cafe ofreció un hermoso escenario para la celebración, con excelente café y deliciosas opciones de desayuno. El evento de corte de cinta y Coffee Connect, al que asistieron numerosos invitados, brindó un comienzo cálido y acogedor al día, reuniendo a miembros de la comunidad para establecer contactos y celebrar la más reciente incorporación al vecindario.

Kaori Cafe y su propietario, Bryan Melendez, recibieron reconocimientos de representantes estatales y locales en conmemoración de la inauguración y como bienvenida a la comunidad empresarial de East Boston. news_communities.title: Most People Cook to Eat, While Others Cook to Feed the Soul
news_communities.author: Times Staff
news_communities.summary:
Story and photos by Al Terminiello Jr. 

Meghann Celona was born in East Boston, and at the early age of 10 moved to Revere with her parents, Jimmy and Loreena Celona. She graduated RHS in 2010, from there, attended the Cordon Bleu Culinary School in Cambridge, then following that, attended the Boston Career Institute to become a Medical Assistant and Phlebotomist. In 2020 Meghann started working at the Beth Isreal Hospital in the ICU.

The real story behind this talented young lady is the fact that she lives an “old school” lifestyle. Learning to cook from her dad, Jimmy Celona. Jimmy was a well know entertainer for many years, singing and bringing joy wherever he performed. That was one side, James Celona was also a decorated veteran and a terrific cook, a skill he passed on to his daughter, Meghann. Meghann recalls the patience and love her dad put into her education as a cook, also the raps on the back of the hand when her concentration veered off the task at hand.

Cooking is a way to bring my dad back to me. Each meal is her way to remember the good old days, a little singing, a little cooking, and a little wine. Each dish has a very specific memory, a time, and a place is recalled with each slice of a knife, or swirl of a spoon. Meghann’s mom, Loreena Celona joins her in the kitchen at times, and new and loving memories are born with each prepared recipe.

These days, Meghann is cooking on a wider platform than family and friends, she has how to do videos on Facebook, Instagram, and Tik Tok. She can be found on Facebook as Meghann Celona, Instagram and Tik Tok @Meghanannxo. Her videos are both entertaining and educational. All recipes are complete with what you need and how to do it. Meghann is also working on a cookbook, while she says it’s a never-ending story of sorts because she continually keeps adding more recipes to it. This project of the heart is a dedication to her dad, it was started before he died and to Meghann, this is a must finish endeavor. Her goal is completion by the fall of 2026, just in time for Christmas sales. Meghann is also in the process of getting sponsors to supply products, to help cut the costs of materials and ingredients for her videos. If you are interested in becoming a sponsor, contact her via email, macelona15@gmail.com, or inbox her on any platform.

Super Bowl Sunday is a big day for her cooking platforms, offering free local delivery from 11am – 2pm/ or pick up all day from 11am. You can order from her online menu and must be placed by January 31. Meghann Celona cooks from the heart, she loves what she does, and it shows in every dish she happily prepares and offers to her friends and family or on camera. Her video catalog was started as an educational home video for her kids to have, for when she is not around, adding modern technology in place of old-fashioned index card recipes. This intimate remembrance for her kids has put Meghann on an national and international stage, with thousands of followers and fans from around the world. She maintains her full-time job. She’s a mother of two, and a partner to her soon-to-be husband. She offers her skills and information to help others enjoy cooking, to find the happiness in their lives by sharing time with loved ones in the kitchen, like she has with her family. Cooking is not a chore, it’s an act of love, and Meghann Celona is bringing that feeling back to families one dish at a time.

news_communities.title: Massport Releases Draft 2025 Strategic Plan; Public Input will Help Shape the Authority’s Vision for the Next Decade
news_communities.author: Times Staff
news_communities.summary:
Special to the Times-Free Press

The Massachusetts Port Authority (Massport) today announced the release of its draft Strategic Plan and is seeking public comment to help shape Massport’s path forward. The Plan builds on Massport’s strengths and a decade of progress, assesses current conditions and trends, and identifies future challenges and opportunities. It balances operational excellence with inclusive forward-looking growth, reaffirming the Authority’s commitment to safe, efficient, sustainable operations that engage and benefit the communities we serve. The Strategic Plan sets a vision for the next decade and outlines a flexible, action-oriented path to achieve it.

The public comment period opens today and runs through March 6th. The Plan and opportunity to provide feedback can be accessed here:  https://www.massport.com/strategic-plan.

“Massport has not undertaken a strategic planning effort in a decade. As a central engine of economic activity, we need to plan for the future and respond to growing demand while setting the highest standards for safety, sustainability and community partnership,” said Rich Davey, Massport CEO. “Guided by this vision, we will be transforming aging infrastructure, leveraging emerging technologies and delivering innovative customer-focused experiences and lasting benefits to our communities and the region. We welcome the public’s feedback as we work to achieve these goals.”

Key elements of the 2025 Plan:

The Strategic Plan presents a vision and a roadmap by key Massport assets and across the organization.

Aviation

Massport’s aviation system must adapt to meet increasing demand and emerging technology as Massachusetts and New England are home to several travel-intensive industries and companies that rely on air travel to support their operations and drive economic impact, as well as a large traveling population that depends on safe, efficient and affordable air service.

Boston Logan International Airport:

With one of the smallest and oldest airport footprints in the country, Logan must invest strategically to maintain safe, efficient, and reliable operations. Priorities include:

A major renovation or replacement of the Air Traffic Control Tower;

Enhancing Boston Logan’s portfolio of high-occupancy vehicle and transit options including implementing innovative ideas like remote terminals;

Modernizing Logan’s terminal core to enhance the passenger experience.

Worcester Regional Airport:

To sustain growth, Worcester will focus on:

Maintaining and expanding commercial passenger service;

Assessing opportunities for potential to grow cargo or logistics operations;

Exploring new opportunities and partnerships with support of community stakeholders.

Hanscom Field

As New England’s premier general aviation airport, Hanscom plays a critical role in supporting business aviation, medical flights, and regional innovation. Priorities include:

Powering the future of aviation innovation with research partnerships and support services;

Advancing Massport’s sustainability and Net Zero efforts, including development and adoption of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF);

Continue to meet metro Boston’s general aviation needs.

Maritime

For nearly 400 years, the Port of Boston has supported regional economic growth and jobs.

Conley Terminal aims to strengthen its position as a reliable, efficient container port with a goal of reaching 200,000 containers by 2035. Achieving best-in-class operational excellence to maximize competitive advantage will require:

Developing a roadmap for crane and wharf upgrades;

Further monetizing and increasing stickiness of customer relationships.

Flynn Cruiseport Boston will focus on improving the passenger experience and enhancing ground access, targeting 600,000 annual passengers by 2035. Priorities include:

Developing core cruise business in collaboration with cruise lines

Improving customer experience and service

Enhancing ground transport access and curbside flow

Real Estate

Massport’s real estate portfolio supports economic growth through waterfront development, maritime industrial uses, and commercial activity. By 2035, Massport aims to generate approximately $100 million in recurring annual revenue. Strategic priorities include:

Attracting and enabling Blue Economy companies;

Funding sustained placemaking activities;

Prioritizing land acquisitions and infrastructure investments.

Organization Wide

Four organization- wide capabilities are essential to delivering on the Plan: Talent, Community, Sustainability & Resilience, and Digital/Data & AI. These are not supporting elements – they are foundational to achieving our mission and advancing Massport’s role as a leader in climate innovation and the region’s sustainability ecosystem.

Our Net Zero strategy continues to be a top priority as we move forward. This strategy addresses all emissions from Massport-controlled sources through sustainable infrastructure and operational improvements. Massport also recognizes the importance of reducing emissions beyond its direct control by extending our influence and building strong partnerships including the adoption of SAF at our airports.

Massport is also committed to advancing the East Boston community’s vision for Piers Park 3. We look forward to working with the community on that.

Massport’s 2025
Strategic Plan is Built on a Decade of Progress

Massport’s 2014 Strategic Plan laid the foundation for major accomplishments across aviation, maritime, and real estate.  Accomplishment achieved from the 2014 Strategic Plan include:

Transformed Conley Container Terminal into a “big-ship-ready” facility capable of handling the largest vessels calling the East Coast;

Additional international gates at Boston Logan’s International Terminal E;

Installed a CAT III landing system at Worcester Airport;

Completed Piers Park II in East Boston;

Delivered a dedicated freight corridor and buffer park in South Boston;

Applied the nationally recognized “Massport Model” to the Omni Boston at the Seaport Hotel procurement;

Opened a new Framingham parking garage that reached full utilization on day one;

Connected Boston  Logan’s terminals beyond security to enhance the passenger experience;

Expanded Boston Logan’s parking capacity;

Maintained Hanscom Field’s role as New England’s premier general aviation airport and a critical reliever for Boston Logan.

These achievements demonstrate Massport’s ability to deliver on complex projects, adapt to evolving needs, and advance economic growth while strengthening community engagement.

news_communities.title: National Grid Offers Safety Reminders for Clearing Snow at Your Home or Business
news_communities.author: Times Staff
news_communities.summary:
Special to the Times-Free Press

National Grid reminds its customers to exercise caution near the energy equipment outside of their homes as they clear snow left behind from Winter Storm Fern. Over a foot of snow has fallen across our Massachusetts service area with additional snow forecast throughout the afternoon. National Grid’s top priority is safety, including the safety and well-being of our crews and customers, and we offer the following reminders to assist with removing snow from your property.

Keep Your Meters Clear of Snow and Ice

Many National Grid customers have electric and/or natural gas meters on the exterior of their homes and businesses. When clearing snow, customers and snow removal contractors should be aware of the location of this equipment to avoid coming into contact with meters or outside gas risers. Additionally:

• Gently remove icicles from the meter with a gloved hand, if necessary. Never kick your meter or use a snow shovel to knock away ice.

• If energy equipment is located near your driveway or parking lot, and you have a vehicle-mounted snowplow or use a snow removal service, ensure that the equipment is well-marked so the vehicle operator knows to keep a safe distance. Striking a natural gas riser or electric wire with a plow could cause injury.

• The buildup of ice and snow around or over natural gas meters, regulators and pipes can pose a serious safety risk. Ice and snow falling from a roof can damage gas meters or service connections to customers’ homes or businesses, resulting in potential gas leaks. Customers should take immediate action if a natural gas leak is suspected:

• Get out – All occupants should leave the house immediately. Do not use the telephone, light switches or automatic garage door openers for any reason.

• Call us – After leaving the house and reaching a safe environment, call National Grid’s 24-hour gas emergency number: 1-800-233-5325.

• Stay out – Don’t return to your home until National Grid tells you it is safe to do so.

• If energy equipment is located near your driveway or parking lot, and you have a vehicle-mounted snowplow or use a snow removal service, ensure that the equipment is well-marked so the vehicle operator knows to keep a safe distance. Striking a natural gas riser or electric wire with a plow could cause injury.

Keep Your Vents Clear

The excessive amount of snowfall from this storm may have increased the snow depth in your yard and near your home or business. When clearing snow from your driveways and walkways, never pile it near vents on the side of the building. A blocked vent – whether it’s a furnace, hot water heater, dryer or sewer – can lead to the buildup of deadly carbon monoxide. Keep the vents clear and free of snow and ice, treating them as carefully as you would an energy meter.

The symptoms of carbon monoxide poisoning are similar to those of the flu, and can include headaches, weakness, confusion, chest tightness, skin redness, dizziness, nausea, sleepiness, heart fluttering or loss of muscle control. If you suspect carbon monoxide is present in your home, immediately go outside and breathe deeply. Then call 911.

Trees, Snow and Your

Electric Service

Snowfall places excessive weight on tree limbs and branches, creating potential hazards for energy infrastructure. Downed trees near power lines should be reported to National Grid at 800-465-1212. Visit our website for more information about your electric service and trees. Customers are also reminded to exercise extreme caution near downed power lines:

• Always use extreme caution near downed power lines and wires, and always assume that they are carrying live electricity.

• Never touch a person or an object that is in contact with a downed line, as electricity can pass through to you.

• Take caution when approaching fallen trees, which could have power lines caught in them.

• Remember that water can conduct electricity. If you see a line down in a puddle or flooded area, avoid contact with the water to prevent risk of shock.

• Learn more about staying safe near downed power lines.

To learn more about electric and natural gas safety, visit our website – nationalgridus.com.

news_communities.title: Dozens displaced as flames spread between buildings in Chelsea
news_communities.summary:
A raging fire swept through a multi-family home in Chelsea, Massachusetts, displacing residents Tuesday evening.

Fire crews responded to the blaze on Cottage Street, finding heavy fire on two floors of the building.

The fire spread to an adjacent building. No one was injured, with Chelsea Fire Chief John Quatieri saying everyone made it out safely.

Authorities initially said seven people were displaced, but La Colaborativa in Chelsea and the American Red Cross of Massachusetts told Telemundo Nueva Inglaterra that 20 adults and six children were displaced. The organizations are assisting them with shelter and aid.

Firefighters faced difficulties at the scene due to the narrow and steep street, and the fact that the buildings are very close together.

Among the damages reported from the fire, the rear staircase collapsed completely, and there are concerns about the roof of the structure. The building was declared a total loss. The adjacent building sustained some damage, but the extent of it was not specified.

Authorities said it appears the fire started on the second floor, and noted that the smoke detectors were working.

The cause of the fire is under investigation.

news_communities.title: Lynn ice rescue highlights common winter danger
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A person fell through the ice at a park in Lynn, Massachusetts, on Wednesday morning and had to be rescued by first responders.

Lynn police said officers were called to Flax Pond at Fecteau Park around 7:30 a.m. The victim was pulled from the water and taken to the hospital in stable condition.

A similar rescue unfolded in Malden, where a young girl fell through the ice at Fellsmere Pond.

Fire officials warned the public to stay off of the ice unless absolutely certain it is thick enough to support the weight.

Ice should be at least 4 inches thick for activities on foot like ice fishing, at least 5 inches for snowmobiles or ATVs, at least 8 to 12 inches for cars or small trucks, and at least 12 to 15 inches for medium trucks, according to the Massachusetts Division of Fisheries and Wildlife.

Moving bodies of water, like creeks and rivers, have higher risk for thin patches.
